elements.sent-2411-001: Equal correspondence to: Shanghang Zhang, Wentao Zhang: wentao.zhang@pku.edu.cn.. \renewcommand\thefootnote{\arabic{footnote \makeatother.}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Current vision-language models (VLMs) show exceptional abilities across diverse tasks including visual question answering.
elements.sent-2411-003: To enhance user experience in practical applications, recent studies investigate VLM personalization to understand user-provided concepts.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, existing studies mainly focus on single-concept personalization, neglecting the existence and interplay of multiple concepts, which limits the real-world applicability of personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we propose the first multi-concept personalization method named MC-LLaVA along with a high-quality multi-concept personalization dataset.
elements.sent-2411-006: Specifically, MC-LLaVA uses a joint training strategy incorporating multiple concepts in a single training step, allowing VLMs to perform accurately in multi-concept personalization.
elements.sent-2411-007: To reduce the cost of joint training, MC-LLaVA leverages visual token information for concept token initialization, yielding improved concept representation and accelerating joint training.
elements.sent-2411-008: To advance multi-concept personalization research, we further contribute a high-quality dataset.
elements.sent-2411-009: We carefully collect images from various movies that contain multiple characters and manually generate the multi-concept question-answer samples.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our dataset features diverse movie types and question-answer types.
elements.sent-2411-011: We conduct comprehensive qualitative and quantitative experiments to demonstrate that MC-LLaVA can achieve impressive multi-concept personalized responses, paving the way for VLMs to become better user-specific assistants.
elements.sent-2411-012: The code and dataset will be publicly available at \href{https://github.com/arctanxarc/MC-LLaVA {https://github.com/arctanxarc/MC-LLaVA .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{fig1_v8.pdf} \caption{The vanilla LLaVA fails to understand user-provided concepts. Existing methods like Yo'LLaVA mainly focus on single-concept personalization and cannot generate accurate personalized responses based on multiple concepts. The proposed MC-LLaVA learns multiple concepts and can perform accurately in multi-concept personalization across various tasks such as recognition, VQA, and caption.} \label{fig:fig1}
elements.sent-2411-013: Over the past few years, large language models (LLMs)~\cite{achiam2023gpt,bai2023qwen,yang2023baichuan,touvron2023llama have made significant advancements, proving their effectiveness in various applications and transforming the way humans interact with machines.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In line with this trend, many vision-language models (VLMs)~\cite{liu2024visual,li2023blip,wang2024visionllm,bai2024survey have been proposed to connect vision encoders with LLMs for various vision-language tasks~\cite{deng2024can,zhou2024mathscape,li2024bigger,lin2024draw such as visual question answering.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite their success, VLMs face challenges when personalized responses are required, such as answering visual questions based on user-provided concepts.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, given images with a concept named $\langle \text{H \rangle$, VLM fails to generate sentences with its identifier, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This limitation hinders the smooth integration of VLMs into our daily lives.
elements.sent-2411-018: Recent personalization methods usually develop a concise representation~\cite{nguyen2024yo for each concept or use additional modules~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm,hao2024remember for personalized concepts.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Although these methods have produced impressive results, they mainly concentrate on single-concept personalization.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, in real-world scenarios, vision-language tasks often involve multiple concepts, which emerges as an essential capability for the practical deployment of personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-021: From this perspective, we find personalizing multiple concepts while ensuring that the responses of VLMs accurately reflect the provided concept information can be challenging for existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, the proposed module in MyVLM~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm only handle single concept scenarios as it can only load one concept embedding per interaction.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo directly learns the concept tokens and classifier head from scratch, exhibiting a strong dependence on high-quality negative samples and slow converge speeds.}
elements.sent-2411-024: As the number of concepts increases, the demand for negative samples rises, making data collection more challenging and requiring model to spend more time fitting the data.
elements.sent-2411-025: Consequently, this leads to significantly higher manual and training costs for fitting these concepts.
elements.sent-2411-026: To solve the problems mentioned above, we introduce a novel multi-concept personalization method called MC-LLaVA, which ensures the accurate generation of personalized responses based on multiple concepts.
elements.sent-2411-027: MC-LLaVA considers multiple concepts together in a single training step rather than treating independently.
elements.sent-2411-028: To reduce the cost of joint training, we pass all concept images through VLM vision encoder and projection layer, using projected vision embeddings to initialize concept tokens.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our experiments show that this initialization can accelerate joint training and reduce dependence on high-quality negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-030: To further achieve the goal of VLM personalization, datasets for training and testing are crucial.
elements.sent-2411-031: Recent studies~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm, nguyen2024yo have constructed datasets for personalized VLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, these datasets are limited to evaluating single concepts.
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, their question-answer types cover only basic recognition and multiple-choice question-answering.
elements.sent-2411-034: The lack of datasets hinders the progress of multi-concept personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, we contribute a high-quality dataset by meticulously gathering images from concept-rich movies.
elements.sent-2411-036: We then utilize GPT-4o~\cite{achiam2023gpt to generate the initial question-answer samples and then manually refine the generated samples.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In total, our dataset includes approximately 1,600 images and corresponding question-answer samples.
elements.sent-2411-038: To comprehensively evaluate multi-concept personalized VLMs, we assess MC-LLaVA across various tasks, including multi-concept recognition, question answering (QA), and captioning.
elements.sent-2411-039: Our dataset will facilitate future research in VLMs personalization.
elements.sent-2411-040: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-041: We introduce MC-LLaVA, the first method designed for multi-concept personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-042: MC-LLaVA is capable of accurately and efficiently learning various concepts and generating personalized responses.
elements.sent-2411-043: We contribute a high-quality dataset for training and testing the multi-concept personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-044: We perform thorough experiments on our dataset and achieve state-of-the-art results among various types of tasks in multi-concept personalization.

elements.sent-2411-045: Following this, the emergence of VLMs~\cite{liu2024visual, lin2023video, bai2023qwenvl, lin2023sphinx has significantly expanded the capabilities of LLMs, enabling them to be applied in tasks such as data processing~\cite{luo2024llm , VQA~\cite{guo2023images , and captioning~\cite{li2023blip .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Although they exhibit strong general abilities in numerous tasks, it is challenging for them to fulfill the requirements for highly personalized responses~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm , especially when involves multiple concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this work, we propose a multi-concept personalization method MC-LLaVA, allowing VLMs to reason accurately with multiple concepts.
elements.sent-2411-048: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning LLMs and VLMs excel in a wide range of downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-049: However, updating and storing all model parameters for each task has become increasingly expensive.
elements.sent-2411-050: Compared to re-train the whole model, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods~\cite{hu2021lora, zhang2024gradient, houlsby2019parameter, zhang2023split, zhang2023mosa achieves training and storage efficiency by updating only a small subset of parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Among PEFT, prompt tuning~\cite{lester2021power,liu2021p, jia2022visual is one of the most widely used methods.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Prompt tuning primarily involves manually designed hard prompts~\cite{wen2024hard and learnable soft prompts~\cite{li2021prefix,zhou2022conditional .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: While soft prompt tuning has achieved notable successes across various tasks, its effectiveness depends on the appropriate initialization of parameters~\cite{meng2024pissa , which leads to current personalization approaches heavily rely on the availability of high-quality negative samples~\cite{nguyen2024yo .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective approach for initializing concept tokens, reducing reliance on negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-055: Additionally, we find this method effectively accelerates convergence speed.
elements.sent-2411-056: Personalized VLMs The emergence of LLMs has revolutionized the way humans interact with machines.
elements.sent-2411-057: Thanks to their superior capabilities in understanding, reasoning, and generation, LLMs serve as fundamental building blocks for our daily lives~\cite{chen2024large,croft2001relevance,salemi2023lamp .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The primary applications include personalized search~\cite{baek2024knowledge,cai2024large and personalized recommendations~\cite{lyu2023llm,zhang2024prospect .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: To enhance individual experiences and preferences, personalized LLMs consider user personas to better meet customized needs.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, in the context of VLMs, personalized models require not only textual information but also additional visual information to aid in understanding concepts.
elements.sent-2411-061: Although recent works~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm,nguyen2024yo, hao2024remember, pi2024personalized have begun to explore VLM personalization, they primarily focus on single-concept scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-062: To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to investigate multi-concept VLM personalization.
elements.sent-2411-063: To promote the advancement of VLM personalization, we create a high-quality dataset that includes both single-concept and multi-concept images along with their corresponding annotations.
elements.sent-2411-064: The overall pipeline of our method is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: We first introduce how to design the joint training strategy for multi-concept scenarios in Sec.~\ref{sec:3.1 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Subsequently, we describe how visual information is used to initialize learnable concept tokens in Sec.~\ref{sec:3.2 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Finally, we present the training details for MC-LLaVA in Sec.~\ref{sec:3.3 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Given a pre-trained VLM, we aim to introduce new concepts to it by using multiple images and their corresponding textual descriptions, allowing the model to produce accurate, personalized responses.
elements.sent-2411-069: Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo employed soft prompt tuning to inject new concepts while preserving the existing knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-070: However, due to the independent training of each concept and the subsequent merging of parameters during inference, they often fail to accurately capture all concepts in multi-concept scenarios, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: To substantiate this observation, we conducted a case study by using concept $\langle \text{bo \rangle$ and $\langle \text{mam \rangle$ from the Yo'LLaVA dataset to create simple multi-concept samples by directly concatenating their test sets.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Results of recognition accuracy can be found in Fig.~\ref{fig:2_mot (left), which showcases the limitation of separately trained methods.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Instead of training separately, we unify all concepts' data, jointly tuning soft prompts and classifier weights (see Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-074: Given \( m \) concepts \( C^1, \ldots, C^m \), each with \( n \) images \( I^1, \ldots, I^n \), we define \( k \) learnable tokens for each concept, These tokens are designed in the following prompt: \begin{equation}\bigcup_{i=1}^{m} \{ \text{``} \langle\text{sks}_i\rangle \ \text{is} \ \langle\text{token}_{(i-1)k+1}\rangle \ldots \langle\text{token}_{ik}\rangle. \text{''} \}\end{equation} Each $\langle \text{sks \rangle$ represents a distinct concept, initialized with unique visual information as detailed in Sec.~\ref{sec:3.2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Due to the introduction of new concepts previously unknown to the VLM, we expand the vocabulary to match the number of concepts, thereby resulting in the final classifier weights $W$, being expanded from $D\times N$ to $D\times (N+m)$, where $D$ denotes the dimensionality of the hidden features and $N$ denotes the original vocabulary size.
elements.sent-2411-076: Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo uses high-quality negative samples that are visually similar to a specific concept but represent non-identical objects for training the concept tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-077: We conduct a case study by reproducing Yo'LLaVA with different percentages of high-quality negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-078: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:2_mot (right), reducing the number of negative samples decreases the personalization capabilities among all kinds of Yo'LLaVA data, reflecting the heavy reliance of Yo'LLaVA on high-quality negative data.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Relying on negative samples creates challenges for multi-concept personalization, as personalizing a new concept necessitates hundreds of high-quality negative samples, which are hard for user to collect.
elements.sent-2411-080: As the number of concepts increases, defining and acquiring high-quality negative samples becomes more challenging.

The left panel illustrates the limitations of separately trained Yo'LLaVA models, while the right panel highlights the importance of high-quality negative samples for Yo'LLaVA.} \label{fig:2_mot}
elements.sent-2411-081: Thus, we explore whether there is a method to quickly capture information about multiple concepts without requiring high-quality negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-082: Utilizing visual information from images to represent concepts is the most efficient approach.
elements.sent-2411-083: For example, describing 'a mischievous blue cat' may connect various cute cats.
elements.sent-2411-084: However, people will not immediately connect this description to the concept $\langle \text{Tom \rangle$, the cat from animated films.}
elements.sent-2411-085: In contrast, upon seeing an image of $\langle \text{Tom \rangle$, one can easily recognize the concept.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Therefore, as shown in the right half of Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 , We propose a method that utilizes concept images to initialize the concept tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-087: For each concept, given a set of images $I^1$,...,$I^n$, We utilize the LLaVA vision encoder $E$ and the projection layer $P$ to obtain aligned visual tokens $P^1$, ..., $P^n$, which have the shape of $n \times (24 \times 24) \times C$.
elements.sent-2411-088: To reduce redundant background information in the visual tokens, we apply the Grounded-SAM~\cite{ren2024grounded with the prompt “the main character in the image” to obtain a mask $M^1$,...,$M^n$ for each image.}
elements.sent-2411-089: After aligning each mask's shape with the visual tokens, we perform an element-wise Hadamard product between $M^i$ and $P^i$.
elements.sent-2411-090: This process yields concept-related visual tokens, denoted as $Q^1$,...,$Q^n$.
elements.sent-2411-091: To obtain a more compact concept representation and reduce the number of visual tokens to $k$, we applied k-means clustering~\cite{hartigan1979algorithm to the concept-related visual tokens, resulting in $K^1$,...,$K^k$, with a size of $k\times C$, where $k$ denotes the final token length corresponding to each concept.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The entire process can be formulated as follows: \begin{equation}K = \text{k-means}\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \{ x \in M^i \odot P(E(I^i)) \mid x \neq 0 \} \right)\end{equation} For the special token $\langle \text{sks \rangle$, we average the clustered $k \times C$ values to create a special token with a shape of $1 \times C$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Ultimately, we derive concept tokens $Q$ with dimensions $(k+1) \times C$, constructed from visual tokens related to the concepts.
elements.sent-2411-094: During experiment, we find that this initialization accelerates convergence, as detailed in Sec.~\ref{exp:epoch , enabling VLMs to be trained efficiently in multi-concept scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-095: To train MC-LLaVA, we need to construct training samples.
elements.sent-2411-096: We leverage a unified training data form ($I$,$X_q$,$X_a$), where $I$ is the input image, $X_q$ is the question, and $X_a$ is the answer.
elements.sent-2411-097: We collect training samples from the following tasks:.
elements.sent-2411-098: Positive recognition: To better integrate concepts into VLMs, we adopt a positive recognition task following Yo'LLaVA, assigning multiple positive recognition conversations to each concept image.
elements.sent-2411-099: Random recognition: To avoid repetitive ``Yes'' responses from the model, we randomly select 100 images from CC12M~\cite{changpinyo2021conceptual as inputs for the negative recognition Task.}
elements.sent-2411-100: These images are paired with conversations generated from a negative recognition template, eliminating the need for visually similar which are convenient to collect.
elements.sent-2411-101: Joint recognition: Joint training not only helps in acquiring effective negative samples for a specific concept but also improves the model's ability to distinguish between concepts through inter-concept negative sampling.
elements.sent-2411-102: Specifically, images from $\langle \text{sks _1 \rangle$ serve as input while negative recognition conversations are generated using $\langle \text{sks _2 \rangle$ from the negative recognition template.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This approach allows for generating at least $m\times(m-1)\times n$ negative samples, given $m$ concepts with $n$ images each.
elements.sent-2411-104: Conversation: Recognition tasks alone do not adequately prepare the model for conversational proficiency; thus, incorporating standard QA samples is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-105: For each concept with n images, we create 10 consistent general questions focusing on visual attributes, with answers provided by GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-106: Notably, while Yo'LLaVA utilizes text-only dialogues for training, we notice that the same concept can exhibit different visual features, such as hairstyles, across various images.
elements.sent-2411-107: Ignoring image information can lead to inconsistencies in quality assurance responses.
elements.sent-2411-108: Furthermore, our experiments indicate that VQA performs better without affecting model's effectiveness in text-only conversations, as demonstrated in Tab.~\ref{exp: main .}
elements.sent-2411-109: Details on the training set construction are provided in Sec.~\ref{sec:4 .}
elements.sent-2411-110: In our multi-concept joint training framework, the parameters to be updated are: \begin{equation}\theta = \{ \langle \text{sks}_{1:m} \rangle, \langle \text{token}_{1:mk} \rangle, W(:, N+1 : N + m) \}\end{equation} We utilize the standard masked language modeling loss to compute the probability of the target responses $X_a$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}(X_a | I, X_q, \theta) = -\sum_{t=1}^{T} \log P(X_{a,t} | I, X_q, X_{a,<t}, \theta)\end{equation} where \( T \) is the length of the answer \( X_a \), \( X_{a,t \) denotes the \( t \)-th word in the answer, and \( P(X_{a,t | I, X_q, X_{a,<t , \theta) \) represents the probability of predicting the \( t \)-th word given the input image \( I \), the question \( X_q \), all preceding words in the answer \( X_{a,<t \), and the parameters \( \theta \).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-111: We find concept token initialization is crucial that misalignment with the tokenizer's embedding distribution can destabilize training.
elements.sent-2411-112: We normalize the vector norms of concept tokens \( K^1, \ldots, K^k \) (denoted \( K_* \)) from k-means.
elements.sent-2411-113: To align with tokenizer embeddings \( K_o \), adjusted tokens are: \begin{equation}\hat{K_*} = \frac{K_*}{||K_*||} \cdot K_o\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-119: There is a lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets in the area of VLM personalization.
elements.sent-2411-120: Recent datasets largely depend on manual photography, which is difficult to obtain in scenarios with multiple concepts.
elements.sent-2411-121: Moreover, user privacy concerns also hinder the scalability of their data collection paradigms.
elements.sent-2411-122: To address the limitations in image data collection, we carefully capture images from various movies that contain multiple characters.
elements.sent-2411-123: This method enables the collection of data with multiple concepts, allowing us to concentrate on instances where several concepts occur together.
elements.sent-2411-124: Detailed statistics of dataset is provided in Tab.~\ref{tab: 2 .}
elements.sent-2411-125: For training, we collect ten images per character, ensuring clear facial features and varied attire and backgrounds for diversity.
elements.sent-2411-126: Testing includes single and multi-concept images; single-concept images follow the training collection process.
elements.sent-2411-127: For multi-concept images, we use predefined character pairs, selecting frames with both characters visible and balanced.
elements.sent-2411-129: After acquiring the training and testing images, we utilize GPT-4o to help generate question-and-answer pairs.
elements.sent-2411-130: For the training images, we first prompt GPT-4o to generate a set of general questions related to the concept.
elements.sent-2411-131: We then manually selected ten questions that provided broad coverage to assist the model in effectively understanding the concept.
elements.sent-2411-132: The chosen images and questions are then input into GPT-4o to receive more refined answers.
elements.sent-2411-133: For the testing images, We utilized GPT-4o to create VQA dialogues and multiple-choice questions that prioritize the images themselves, rather than the general concept-related questions found in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-134: Specifically, if a testing image contains only one concept, the questions focus solely on that concept.
elements.sent-2411-135: Meanwhile, for images that contain multiple concepts, the questions incorporate both single-concept and multi-concept queries to ensure the evaluation is more reflective of real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-136: The questions are generated by GPT-4o and manually selected.
elements.sent-2411-137: Then, we input the images and prompts into GPT-4o, using the outputs as ground truth for evaluating models.
elements.sent-2411-138: To construct our dataset, we query GPT-4o approximately 100K times.

elements.sent-2411-144: We compare our MC-LLaVA with naive prompting strategies and other VLM personalization methods:.
elements.sent-2411-145: LLaVA: Vanilla LLaVA~\cite{liu2024visual without any personalized information provided;.}
elements.sent-2411-146: LLaVA+Prompt: We first prompt LLaVA to generate captions for all training images of the concept, and then utilize these personalized captions in two ways: (I) We concatenate all captions to create a comprehensive caption of the concept.
elements.sent-2411-147: During inference, we add $m$ relevant captions to the input to provide information specific to the concept being tested, where $m$ represents number of concepts to be evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-148: Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo : We perform separate training for each concept.}
elements.sent-2411-149: It is important to note that we engage in text-only conversations, in accordance with established literature.
elements.sent-2411-150: During testing, we combine the concept token corresponding to each concept with the extended parameters in the language model head.
elements.sent-2411-151: GPT4o+Prompt: Similar to LLaVA+Prompt, but changing base model to GPT-4o, which acts as the upper bound among downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-152: Notably, GPT4o used for testing differs from the one employed for data generation, avoiding the risk of knowledge leakage.

elements.sent-2411-170: In addition to identifying specific concepts, VLMs need to perform question-answering (QA) in personalized scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-171: We assessed the QA capabilities of MC-LLaVA by evaluating it on two types of tasks: choice-based QA (both visual and text-only) and visual question answering (VQA).
elements.sent-2411-172: Following Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo , we developed multiple-choice questions for scenarios with different numbers of concepts, including 465 questions for single-concept scenarios and 872 for multi-concept scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-173: While choice-based QA might not fully reflect the model's reasoning ability due to guesswork, we also created an equivalent number of complete VQA pairs to further assess the model's question-answering proficiency based on its generated responses.
elements.sent-2411-174: In choice-based QA, we assess model's accuracy in selecting the correct option.
elements.sent-2411-175: For VQA, we use BLEU~\cite{papineni2002bleu to evaluate similarity between model's predictions and generated ground truth.}
elements.sent-2411-176: MC-LLaVA shows significantly better performance in visual QA(VQA) tasks, with a 23.3\% improvement over Yo'LLaVA in choice-based QA, achieving results comparable to GPT-4o when utilizing the same number of tokens.
elements.sent-2411-177: Despite implementing the joint training strategy, Yo'LLaVA's reliance on text-only data for training may limit its performance in complex, visually related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-178: In the standard VQA setting, our method achieved an overall BLEU score of 0.652, ranking second to GPT-4o and demonstrating a 6.4\% improvement over Yo'LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-179: Both the standard LLaVA and LLaVA with personalized prompts exhibited suboptimal performance in the VQA task.
elements.sent-2411-180: As previously discussed in choice-based QA, Yo'LLaVA's reliance exclusively on text-only training data is a key factor in its inferior performance on VQA tasks.
elements.sent-2411-181: In the text-only QA task, our MC-LLaVA demonstrates performance that is competitive with Yo'LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-182: However, Yo'LLaVA is trained exclusively on text data, while our MC-LLaVA starts with visual tokens, which may influence its performance on text-based tasks.
elements.sent-2411-183: Yo'LLaVA, which uses a joint training strategy and is trained on only text data, achieves the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-184: In text-based tasks, there is a direct correlation between the number of prompt tokens and model performance, as shown by the significant improvement in performance with around 500 tokens in the LLaVA+Prompt approach.

elements.sent-2411-205: We present MC-LLaVA, a novel multi-concept personalized vision-language model that greatly improves the accuracy and efficiency of personalized responses in multi-concept scenarios through joint training strategy and concept token initialization.
elements.sent-2411-206: Our work not only advances the frontiers of VLM personalization but also offers a high-quality multi-concept dataset for future studies in the field.
elements.sent-2411-207: The excellent performance of MC-LLaVA in various types of tasks, such as recognition, question answering, and captioning, highlights its ability to generate personalized responses on user-provided concepts.

elements.sent-2411-221: In our work, we meticulously select character-rich frames from various video to construct a customized dataset.

elements.sent-2411-223: However, there is a potential issue that the Visual Language Model (VLM) itself may have prior knowledge of certain concepts.
elements.sent-2411-224: Consequently, we conducted an in-depth exploration and discussion.

elements.sent-2411-246: Recognition We consider a scenario consisting of $n$ concepts.
elements.sent-2411-247: To evaluate the model's recognition ability for single concept images, where each concept has 5 test images only featuring the concept.
elements.sent-2411-248: Each of these 5$n$ images is queried with n concepts, resulting in 5$n$ positive and 5$n(n-1)$ negative test samples.
elements.sent-2411-249: Additionally, we randomly select 50 external single concept images as negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-250: For multi concept image recognition, each scenario includes 5 test images, where each containing up to $n$ concepts.
elements.sent-2411-251: We query the model on the presence of each concept and all concepts collectively, yielding up to 5$(n+1)$ positive samples.
elements.sent-2411-252: We further select 50 external multi concept images, querying them with several of the $n$ concepts for negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-253: In total, the method is assessed on up to 5$n^2$ + 5$(n+1)$ + 100 recognition tasks, comprising 5$n$ + 5$(n+1)$ positive and 100 + 5$n(n-1)$ negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-254: Question answering All visual tasks share same data composition.
elements.sent-2411-255: For a scenario with $n$ concepts, each of the 5 single concept test images contributes 5 QA pairs, resulting in 5$n$ QA pairs for single concept images.
elements.sent-2411-256: Each of the 5 multi concept images, assuming they contain all n concepts, generates $2^n - 1$ QA pairs (corresponding to the non-empty subsets of an n-element set).
elements.sent-2411-257: To sum up, for each task, a scenario with n concepts can have up to $5(n+2^n-1)$ QA pairs for testing.
elements.sent-2411-258: For text-only QA, a n-concept scenario contains 5$n$ single concept QA pairs and 5 multi concept QA pairs, resulting in $5n+5$ text-only QA pairs for testing.
elements.sent-2411-259: Captioning In an n-concept scenario, all test images—comprising 5$n$ single-concept images and 5 multi-concept images—are utilized for concept experiments.
elements.sent-2411-260: We prompt the model to generate captions for each image and quantitatively assess the model's captioning capability based on the presence of identifiers within the images.
elements.sent-2411-261: This methodology provides a metric for evaluating the model's ability to accurately caption and recognize concepts in both single and multi-concept settings.
elements.sent-2411-262: We use LLaVA-1.5-13B~\cite{liu2024visual as the VLM backbone for all experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-263: During training, the $n$ concepts within a single scenario are jointly trained in one pass.
elements.sent-2411-264: For each concept, all 10 images from the training set are used, with a batch size of 1.
elements.sent-2411-265: Training is conducted for 15 epochs, and at the end of the final epoch, we save the token embeddings corresponding to each concept and the parameters of the LM head.
elements.sent-2411-266: The training process consists of two phases.
elements.sent-2411-267: First, we initialize the parameters using $k$-means clustering~\cite{hartigan1979algorithm with euclidean distance.}
elements.sent-2411-268: Then, during the VQA-based training phase, we optimize the model using AdamW~\cite{kingma2014adam with a learning rate of 0.001, applying the standard masked language modeling (MLM) loss.}
elements.sent-2411-269: Each epoch consists of an average of $250 + 100(n-1)$ steps per concept.
elements.sent-2411-270: For testing, due to the stochastic nature of language model generation and the randomness in test set composition (especially for recognition tasks), each test task is performed three times using the same three fixed seeds for all scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-271: The average result across the three runs is reported.
elements.sent-2411-272: All experiments are conducted on 80GB A100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-273: During the concept training phase, we follow the positive and negative recognition templates described in Yo’LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo , using 30 positive and 30 negative templates, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Specifically, for each positive training image, we randomly select 5 QA pairs from the positive recognition templates due to the limited number of positive images.
elements.sent-2411-275: For the joint negative recognition images, we select 10 QA pairs per image from the negative recognition templates.
elements.sent-2411-276: In contrast, for randomly selected images, we only assign one QA pair from the templates.
elements.sent-2411-277: This ensures balanced and diverse training samples while leveraging the templates effectively.

elements.sent-2411-290: MC-LLaVA enhances the capacity for personalized interactions in vision-language models, particularly excelling in multi-concept scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-291: However, it is essential to acknowledge several limitations.
elements.sent-2411-292: Firstly, while MC-LLaVA leverages visual information to facilitate the accurate and efficient integration of new concepts into VLMs, the current process still necessitates training, which poses certain challenges for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-293: A promising avenue for future research is to explore the possibility of integrating new concepts to the model without training.
elements.sent-2411-294: Secondly, while our multi-concept dataset pioneers task-level evaluation in VLM personalization, the field lacks comprehensive benchmarks that encompass a larger scale and capability dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-295: This limitation restricts our assessment of capability-level VLM personalization.
elements.sent-2411-296: Future work could define the capabilities that models should be evaluated on and propose more comprehensive benchmarks that assess various aspects of personalization capabilities in VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-297: Current vision-language models (VLMs) show exceptional abilities across diverse tasks, such as visual question answering.
elements.sent-2411-298: To enhance user experience, recent studies investigate VLM personalization to understand user-provided concepts.
elements.sent-2411-299: However, they mainly focus on single-concept personalization, neglecting the existence and interplay of multiple concepts, which limits real-world applicability.
elements.sent-2411-300: This paper proposes the first multi-concept personalization paradigm, MC-LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-301: Specifically, MC-LLaVA employs a multi-concept instruction tuning strategy, effectively integrating multiple concepts in a single training step.
elements.sent-2411-302: To reduce the costs related to joint training, we propose a personalized textual prompt that uses visual token information to initialize concept tokens.
elements.sent-2411-303: Additionally, we introduce a personalized visual prompt during inference, aggregating location confidence maps for enhanced recognition and grounding capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-304: To advance multi-concept personalization research, we further contribute a high-quality instruction tuning dataset.
elements.sent-2411-305: We carefully collect images with multiple characters and objects from movies and manually generate question-answer samples for multi-concept scenarios, featuring superior diversity.
elements.sent-2411-306: Comprehensive qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that MC-LLaVA can achieve impressive multi-concept personalized responses, paving the way for VLMs to become better user-specific assistants.
elements.figure-2411-028: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{image/2_mot.pdf} \caption{Case studies utilizing various concepts from the Yo’LLaVA dataset. The left panel shows the limitations of separately trained Yo'LLaVA models, while the right panel emphasizes the significance of high-quality negative samples for Yo'LLaVA.} \label{fig:2_mot}
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{image/iccv_intro_compressed.pdf} \caption{The vanilla LLaVA fails to understand user-provided concepts. Existing methods like Yo'LLaVA mainly focus on single-concept personalization and cannot generate accurate, personalized responses based on multi-concepts. The proposed MC-LLaVA learns multiple concepts and can perform accurately in multi-concept personalization across various tasks such as recognition, VQA, and captioning.} \label{fig:fig1}
elements.sent-2411-307: For example, given images with a concept named $\langle \text{Anna \rangle$, VLMs fail to generate sentences with its identifier, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-308: Although some methods~\cite{nguyen2024yo,alaluf2025myvlm,hao2024remember have produced impressive results in VLM personalization, they mainly concentrate on single-concept personalization.}
elements.sent-2411-309: However, in real-world scenarios, vision-language tasks often involve multiple concepts, which is crucial for the effective deployment of personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-310: From this perspective, personalizing multiple concepts while ensuring that VLMs respond accurately can be challenging for current methods.
elements.sent-2411-311: For example, Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo faces two main challenges when directly applied to multi-concept scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-312: First, because it trains each concept separately, merging parameters for different concepts leads to severe performance degradation due to concepts confusion (see Fig.~\ref{fig:2_mot (1)).}
elements.sent-2411-313: Second, Yo'LLaVA learns concept tokens and classifier heads from scratch, resulting in a strong reliance on high-quality negative samples (see Fig.~\ref{fig:2_mot (2)).}
elements.sent-2411-314: As the number of concepts increases, the demand for negative samples also rises, making data collection more challenging and requiring the model to spend additional time fitting the data.
elements.sent-2411-315: Consequently, multi-concept personalization of VLMs incurs significantly higher manual labor and training costs.
elements.sent-2411-316: To solve the abovementioned problems, we introduce a novel method called MC-LLaVA, which ensures the accurate generation of personalized responses based on multiple concepts.
elements.sent-2411-317: MC-LLaVA considers multiple concepts together in a single training step rather than treating them independently.
elements.sent-2411-318: To reduce the cost of joint training, we pass all concept images through the VLM vision encoder and projection layer, using projected vision embeddings to initialize the concept tokens in personalized textual prompts.
elements.sent-2411-319: Additionally, MC-LLaVA enhances the model's recognition and grounding capabilities by introducing a personalized visual prompt.
elements.sent-2411-320: We aggregate the location confidence maps based on the concept tokens to create the personalized visual prompt for VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-321: To advance research in multi-concept personalization, datasets for training and testing are essential.
elements.sent-2411-322: Recent studies~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm,nguyen2024yo have developed datasets for personalized VLMs; however, these datasets focus only on evaluating single concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-323: Furthermore, the types of questions and answers they address are limited to basic recognition and multiple-choice formats.
elements.sent-2411-324: In total, our dataset includes approximately 2,000 images and 16,700 question-answer samples.
elements.sent-2411-325: To comprehensively evaluate multi-concept personalized VLMs, we assess MC-LLaVA across various tasks, including multi-concept recognition, visual grounding, question answering (QA), and captioning.
elements.sent-2411-326: Our dataset will facilitate future research in VLM personalization.
elements.sent-2411-327: We introduce MC-LLaVA, the first method designed for multi-concept personalized VLMs, which employs personalized textual and visual prompts to learn various concepts and generate tailored responses effectively.
elements.sent-2411-328: We contribute a high-quality dataset for training and testing multi-concept personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-329: We perform thorough experiments on our dataset—as well as on two additional benchmarks—achieving state-of-the-art results among various types of tasks in single- and multi-concept personalization.
elements.figure-2411-030: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{image/iccv_main_final.pdf} \caption{The illustration of MC-LLaVA. (a) We use a multi-concept joint training strategy to learn the personalized textual prompts and classifier weights. (b) Given $m$ concepts, we utilize visual tokens obtained from K-means centroids to initialize the $m \times (k+1)$ concept tokens in personalized textual prompts, reducing the costs associated with joint training. (c) During inference, we introduce a personalized visual prompt for VLMs by aggregating location confidence maps based on learned concept tokens. } \label{fig:fig2}
elements.sent-2411-330: Recently, LLMs have achieved significant advancements~\cite{achiam2023gpt,touvron2023llama,bai2023qwen, yang2023baichuan .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Following this, the emergence of VLMs~\cite{liu2024visual,lin2023video, bai2023qwenvl,lin2023sphinx has significantly expanded the capabilities of LLMs, enabling them to be applied in tasks such as data processing~\cite{luo2024llm , VQA~\cite{guo2023images , and captioning~\cite{li2023blip .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-332: Although they exhibit strong general abilities in numerous tasks, it is challenging for them to fulfill the requirements for highly personalized responses~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm , especially when multiple concepts are involved.}
elements.sent-2411-333: In this work, we propose a multi-concept personalization method, allowing VLMs to reason accurately with multiple concepts.. Personalized VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-334: The emergence of LLMs has revolutionized the way humans interact with machines.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our method's pipeline is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-336: First, we propose multi-concept instruction tuning for MC-LLaVA (Sec.~\ref{sec:mc_tune ).}
elements.sent-2411-337: Next, we introduce a personalized textual prompt for multi-concept (Sec.~\ref{sec:textualprompt ).}
elements.sent-2411-338: Finally, we propose a personalized visual prompt to boost recognition and grounding (Sec.~\ref{sec:visualprompt ).}
elements.sent-2411-339: Given a pre-trained VLM and multiple user-provided concepts, our goal is to introduce new concepts by expanding the vocabulary and learning the personalized textual prompts, while preserving the model’s knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-340: Instead of training concepts separately, as in Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo , we propose a joint training approach that considers multiple concepts together and simultaneously learns personalized textual prompts and classifier weights (see Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 (a)).}}
elements.sent-2411-341: Specifically, for $m$ concepts $\{C^j\}_{j=1 ^m$, each with $n$ images $\{I^i\}_{i=1 ^n$, we define $k+1$ learnable tokens per concept: \begin{equation}\bigcup_{j=1}^{m} \{\langle\text{sks}_j\rangle, \ \langle\text{token}_{(j-1)k+1}\rangle \ldots \langle\text{token}_{jk}\rangle\} \label{eq:ptp}\end{equation} where each $\langle \text{sks _j \rangle$ serves as a unique concept identifier.}}}
elements.sent-2411-342: As these concepts are new to the VLM, we expand the vocabulary by adjusting the language model classifier's weights $W$ from $D \times N$ to $D \times (N+m)$, where $D$ is the feature dimension and $N$ is the original vocabulary size.
elements.sent-2411-343: To train MC-LLaVA, we construct training samples in the form $(I, X_q, X_a)$, where $I$ is the input image, $X_q$ is the question, and $X_a$ is the answer.
elements.sent-2411-344: Following prior works such as Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo , we adopt standard dialogue formats (e.g., positive recognition, random recognition, and conversation tasks) in our training pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-345: Additionally, we design a novel joint recognition task.
elements.sent-2411-346: In our joint training, we pair text and image samples corresponding to different concepts within the same scenario, to generate cross-concept pairs.
elements.sent-2411-347: This inter-concept negative sampling strategy produces at least $m\times(m-1)\times n$ negative samples for a scenario containing $m$ concepts with $n$ images each.
elements.sent-2411-348: All the mentioned data examples are shown in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-349: To train the newly introduced tokens, we construct the personalized system prompt by sequentially appending prompts for each concept in the given scenario.
elements.sent-2411-350: Specifically, for each concept $C^j$, we add the prompt: ``$\langle \text{sks _j \rangle$ is $\langle \text{token _{(j-1)k+1 \rangle \dots \langle \text{token _{jk \rangle$''.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-351: Furthermore, to decrease reliance on high-quality negative samples, which are challenging to acquire in multi-concept scenarios, we present a new initialization strategy that leverages visual information.
elements.sent-2411-352: Instead of manually collecting negative samples to learn the personalized textual prompts in Eq.~\ref{eq:ptp , we directly extract visual tokens from concept images to initialize concept tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-353: Given a set of training images $\{I^i\}_{i=1 ^n$ for each concept, we utilize the LLaVA vision encoder $E_{\text{CLIP }$ and the projection layer $P_{\text{MM }$ to obtain the projected visual tokens $\{F_{\text{MM ^i\}_{i=1 ^{nhw}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-354: To eliminate background noise, we apply Grounded-SAM~\cite{ren2024grounded with the prompt “the main character in the image” to generate masks $\{M^i\}_{i=1 ^n$ as a preprocessing step conducted offline.}}
elements.sent-2411-355: The concept-relevant tokens $\{\tilde{F _{\text{MM ^i\}_{i=1 ^{l}$ are then extracted through an element-wise Hadamard product between $\{F_{\text{MM ^i\}_{i=1 ^{nhw}$ and their corresponding masks.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-356: To construct a compact concept representation, we apply k-means clustering~\cite{hartigan1979algorithm to the compressed visual tokens, reducing them to $k$ cluster centers $\{K^i\}_{i=1 ^{k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-357: The special token $\langle \text{sks \rangle$ is then computed as the mean of these clustered centers, yielding a representation of shape $1 \times D$.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Consequently, the final concept tokens for each concept have dimensions $(k+1) \times D$, effectively encapsulating the concept’s semantic essence.
elements.sent-2411-359: As demonstrated in Sec.~\ref{exp:epoch , this initialization significantly accelerates convergence, facilitating efficient multi-concept training in VLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-360: Localizing concepts enhances a model's recognition and grounding in multi-concept scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-361: Relying solely on textual tokens may be inadequate for accurate recognition and grounding.
elements.sent-2411-362: To address this, we propose Personalized Visual Prompt based on Set-of-Mark (SOM)~\cite{yang2023set , an inference-time, training-free method that provides additional spatial information about concepts without introducing extra modules.}
elements.sent-2411-363: The construction of the personalized visual prompt consists of two key stages: generating the location confidence map and constructing the visual prompt.
elements.sent-2411-364: Consider a multi-concept scenario with $m$ concepts.
elements.sent-2411-365: During training, for each concept $C^j$, we store a set of filtered features $\{\tilde{F _{\text{CLIP ^i\}_{i=1 ^{l_{C^j }$, obtained by applying the LLaVA vision encoder $E_{\text{CLIP }$ and subsequent masking to the training images.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: Given a test image $I_t$, we can also extract its encoded features $F_t \in \mathbb{R ^{hw \times c}$ using $E_{\text{CLIP }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-367: For each concept $C^j$, we compute the cosine similarity between feature set $\{\tilde{F _{\text{CLIP ^i\}_{i=1 ^{l_{C^j }$ and test image feature $F_t$: \begin{equation}S^i_{C^j} = \frac{F_t \tilde{F}_{\text{CLIP}}^{i\text{T}}}{\|\tilde{F}_{\text{CLIP}}^i\|_2 \cdot \|F_t\|_2}, \quad i = 1, \dots, l_{C^j}\end{equation} where $S^i_{C^j}$ represents the similarity map between the test image and the $i$-th stored feature of concept $C^j$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-368: This results in a set of similarity maps $\{S^i_{C^j}\}_{i=1 ^{l_{C^j }$, where each $S^i_{C^j}$ describes the probability distribution of different local parts of $C^j$ in the test image.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: To obtain a robust location confidence map, we first aggregate the similarity maps using average pooling: \begin{equation}\tilde{S}_{C^j} = \frac{1}{l_{C^j}} \sum_{i=1}^{l_{C^j}} S^i_{C^j} - \frac{1}{|C|} \sum_{j=1}^{|C|} \left( \frac{1}{l_{C^j}} \sum_{i=1}^{l_{C^j}} S^i_{C^j} \right)\end{equation} where $|C|$ is the total number of concepts.
elements.sent-2411-370: The first term represents the mean similarity map for concept $C^j$, while the second term is the global mean similarity map across all concepts.
elements.sent-2411-371: By subtracting the global mean, we eliminate systematic biases caused by different activation levels.
elements.sent-2411-372: This ensures that concept detection relies on relative similarity instead of absolute similarity values.
elements.sent-2411-373: We determine each concept $ C^j$'s existence and localization in the test image.
elements.sent-2411-374: The existence of $C^j$ is verified by checking whether a sufficiently large proportion of pixels in $\tilde{S _{C^j}$ exceed a confidence threshold $\tau$.}
elements.sent-2411-375: If this proportion surpasses a predefined minimum presence ratio $\gamma$, we consider $C^j$ present.
elements.sent-2411-376: The representative pixel location is then selected as the coordinate with the highest confidence in $\tilde{S _{C^j}$.}
elements.sent-2411-377: Otherwise, no visual prompt is marked for this concept.
elements.sent-2411-378: Finally, we aggregate the existence and location information of all detected concepts to create the SOM.
elements.sent-2411-379: For the detected $\tilde{m}$ concepts $\{C^j\}_{j=1 ^{\tilde{m }$ in the test image, we append the following system prompt: $\langle\text{sks _j\rangle \text{ is located at ``Mark Number j \text{" .$ This visual prompt provides localization information to the model, enhancing recognition and grounding in multi-concept scenarios.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-380: In this section, we provide a comprehensive explanation of the process involved in creating our multi-concept instruction dataset.
elements.sent-2411-381: The dataset includes a training set with single-concept images and a testing set containing both single- and multi-concept images, totaling approximately 2.0K images.
elements.sent-2411-382: Our dataset is superior due to its support for multiple concepts, more advanced captions, and a larger sample size.

elements.sent-2411-383: The field of VLM personalization lacks large-scale, high-quality datasets.
elements.sent-2411-384: Existing datasets rely mainly on manually captured photos, which are challenging to obtain in multi-concept scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-385: Moreover, privacy concerns hinder the scalability of such data collection.
elements.sent-2411-386: To overcome these limitations, we systematically curate images from a diverse selection of animated and live-action films worldwide, ensuring broad cultural and artistic coverage.
elements.sent-2411-387: This approach facilitates the collection of multi-concept data, focusing on instances where multiple concepts co-occur.
elements.sent-2411-388: To prevent the model from relying on pre-trained knowledge for concept recognition, we reassign each concept a generic label (e.g., $\langle \text{Anna \rangle$).}
elements.sent-2411-389: Our dataset encompasses a wide range of concepts, including animals, human characters, and objects, providing a rich and diverse resource for model training.
elements.sent-2411-390: Detailed dataset statistics are provided in Tab.~\ref{tab:2 .}
elements.sent-2411-391: For training, we collect ten images per concept, ensuring distinct visual characteristics with diverse appearances, contexts, and backgrounds to enhance generalization.
elements.sent-2411-392: The test set includes both single- and multi-concept images.
elements.sent-2411-393: Single-concept images follow the same collection strategy as the training set.
elements.sent-2411-394: For multi-concept images, we define specific pairs and select frames where all concepts are clearly visible, maintaining a balanced distribution.
elements.sent-2411-395: To ensure fairness and high data quality, the data collection process was collaboratively designed by a team of ten members, comprising university students and researchers from technology companies, minimizing subjectivity and bias.
elements.sent-2411-396: The upper section of Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset presents sampled images from the dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-397: After acquiring the training and testing images, we employ GPT-4o to generate question-answer pairs.
elements.sent-2411-398: For the training images, we first prompt GPT-4o to generate a diverse set of general questions related to each concept.
elements.sent-2411-399: Subsequently, we manually select ten questions per concept that provide broad coverage, ensuring the model can effectively learn the concept.
elements.sent-2411-400: These selected images and questions are then input into GPT-4o to generate more refined answers.
elements.sent-2411-401: For the testing images, we utilize GPT-4o to create VQA dialogues and multiple-choice questions that prioritize the visual content of the images rather than the broader concept-related questions used in the training set.
elements.sent-2411-402: Specifically, if a test image contains only a single concept, the questions focus solely on that concept.
elements.sent-2411-403: Conversely, for images containing multiple concepts, the questions include both single-concept and multi-concept queries to ensure a more realistic evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-404: The generated questions are manually curated to maintain quality, after which the images and prompts are fed into GPT-4o to obtain answers, which serve as ground truth for model evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-405: To construct our dataset, we queried GPT-4o approximately 100K times.
elements.sent-2411-406: The lower section of Fig.~\ref{fig:dataset presents examples of training and testing data.}

elements.sent-2411-410: Finally, ablations in Sec.~\ref{sec:5.5 confirm our method's efficiency and effectiveness.}

elements.sent-2411-427: To evaluate the model's recognition ability, we conduct experiments on the MC-LLaVA, Yo'LLaVA, and MyVLM datasets.
elements.sent-2411-428: For the latter two, we adhere to the evaluation protocols specified in their respective papers.
elements.sent-2411-429: For our dataset, images containing the queried concept are treated as positive examples, whereas negative examples are selected from images depicting other concepts in the same scenario as well as from unrelated scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-430: Details are provided in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-431: Each test uses the query: ``Can you see {$\langle \text{sks _i \rangle$} in this photo? Answer with a single word: Yes or No.'' Test data is categorized as single-concept or multi-concept based on the number of concepts queried in each question.}
elements.sent-2411-432: To mitigate potential sample imbalance, we follow Yo'LLaVA~\cite{nguyen2024yo and report the arithmetic mean of the yes and no recall for positive samples and negative samples.}
elements.sent-2411-433: As summarized in Tab.~\ref{exp:rec , Vanilla LLaVA achieves the lowest scores, because it lacks any additional concept information.}
elements.sent-2411-434: Moreover, simply adding personalized prompts (LLaVA+P) results in only marginal improvements, suggesting that only a textual prompt does not effectively personalize LLaVA.
elements.sent-2411-435: Yo'LLaVA-M, without leveraging visual features, exhibits reduced performance on both single- and multi-concept queries, possibly due to confusion between different concepts.
elements.sent-2411-436: RAP-MLLM employs extra recognition modules and supports multi-concept queries by using top-K selection mechanism, however, this approach may occasionally struggle to accurately detect when a concept is absent.
elements.sent-2411-437: In comparison, our proposed MC-LLaVA method uses fewer tokens and achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) recognition performance in both single- and multi-concept scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-438: Notably, MC-LLaVA outperforms GPT4o+P, demonstrating its integrated textual and visual prompt design delivers richer concept-specific information than GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-439: To further assess the model's visual grounding capability, particularly in multi-concept scenarios, we manually annotate the locations of each concept in multi-concept images.
elements.sent-2411-440: The model is then tested using a multiple-choice format to determine each concept’s position: ``Where is {$\langle \text{sks _i \rangle$} located in this photo? A.. Left.}
elements.sent-2411-441: Right.'' The results are reported in terms of accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-442: As shown in Tab.~\ref{exp:rec , because the visual grounding task further evaluates the model’s ability to localize specific concepts in multi-concept scenarios, most models exhibit a slight performance drop compared to the recognition task.}
elements.sent-2411-443: Notably, RAP-MLLM secures the second-best performance, likely due to its pre-training data incorporating grounding-related tasks that enhance localization capabilities, whereas MC-LLaVA, with a well-designed visual prompt, achieves SOTA performance.

elements.sent-2411-444: In addition to recognizing specific concepts, VLMs must demonstrate question-answering (QA) capabilities in personalized scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-445: We evaluate QA performance on two datasets: our proposed dataset and Yo'LLaVA dataset~\cite{nguyen2024yo .}
elements.sent-2411-446: For Yo'LLaVA, we utilize their publicly available 571 multiple-choice QA test samples.
elements.sent-2411-447: Our dataset evaluation employs two complementary approaches: (1) multiple-choice QA covering both visual and text-based questions and (2) open-ended visual question answering (VQA).
elements.sent-2411-448: For visual questions, we construct multiple-choice questions across different concept configurations, including single- and multi-concept questions for composite scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-449: The dataset contains 1,180 single-concept and 600 multi-concept multiple-choice questions.
elements.sent-2411-450: To mitigate the influence of random guessing in multiple-choice questions, we create corresponding VQA pairs (equal in number to the multiple-choice questions) for comprehensive evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-451: We employ two evaluation metrics: accuracy for choice selection and BLEU~\cite{papineni2002bleu for text in open-ended responses.}
elements.sent-2411-452: As shown in Tab.~\ref{exp:vqa , MC-LLaVA achieves significantly improved performance in visual question answering (VQA), with results comparable to GPT-4o.}
elements.sent-2411-453: In the multiple-choice QA evaluation, MC-LLaVA delivers competitive performance with GPT-4o and outperforms all other baselines.
elements.sent-2411-454: In the open-ended VQA setting, our method attains an overall BLEU score of 0.658, ranking second only to GPT-4o.
elements.sent-2411-455: Notably, RAP-MLLM, which is pre-trained on large scale of personalized data, tends to generate shorter responses and consequently scores the lowest in BLEU.
elements.sent-2411-456: To evaluate whether the language model has truly memorized the new concepts, we designed 590 single-concept and 250 multi-concept text-only multiple-choice questions that focus on each concept's intrinsic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-457: In the text-only QA task (Tab.~\ref{exp:vqa ), LLaVA+Prompt shows a notable performance boost as the number of prompt tokens increases, thanks to the enriched textual context.}
elements.sent-2411-458: Among all models, MC-LLaVA achieves SOTA performance.
elements.sent-2411-459: We conduct captioning evaluations on both our dataset and the MyVLM~\cite{alaluf2025myvlm dataset, following the evaluation methodology proposed by MyVLM to compute captioning recall.}
elements.sent-2411-460: The metric's detailed calculation method and the prompt are provided in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-461: As shown in Tab.~\ref{exp:vqa , on the MC-LLaVA dataset, our method achieves a weighted captioning recall of 0.754—outperforming most baselines—although it remains slightly below GPT4o+P.}
elements.sent-2411-462: On the MyVLM dataset, our approach attains a captioning recall of 0.959, nearly matching GPT4o and exceeding other models.

elements.sent-2411-468: As shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:module , starting with Yo'LLaVA without high-quality negative samples (HNS), we sequentially evaluate our three core techniques: joint training and token initialization at the text token level, and visual prompting for enhanced recognition and grounding.}
elements.sent-2411-469: Incorporating HNS significantly improves concept recognition, visual grounding, choice-based tasks, and captioning, while VQA remains less affected—likely due to BLEU’s sensitivity to extra text.
elements.sent-2411-470: Overall, these techniques yield substantial gains, with visual prompting delivering the largest improvements in recognition and grounding, and joint training better preserving language generation in VQA compared to direct parameter concatenation.
elements.sent-2411-471: We present MC-LLaVA, a novel multi-concept personalized vision-language model that significantly improves accuracy and efficiency via multi-concept instruction tuning equipped with personalized textual prompt and personalized visual prompt.
elements.sent-2411-472: Our work not only advances frontiers of VLM personalization but also offers a high-quality multi-concept instruction dataset for future research.
elements.sent-2411-473: MC-LLaVA’s excellent performance across multiple tasks among various benchmarks highlights its ability to generate personalized responses based on user-provided concepts.
elements.sent-2411-474: With growing demand for personalized services, MC-LLaVA and its dataset provide a strong foundation for developing more intelligent and user-specific assistants.
elements.sent-2411-475: This advancement further paves the way for new opportunities in real-world applications and transformed how we interact with assistants.

elements.sent-2411-482: LLMs and VLMs excel in a wide range of downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-483: In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective approach for initializing concept tokens, which reduces reliance on negative samples.
elements.sent-2411-484: Additionally, we find that this method effectively accelerates the convergence speed.
elements.sent-2411-486: Positive Recognition: To better integrate concepts into VLMs, we adopt a positive recognition task following Yo'LLaVA, assigning multiple positive recognition conversations to each concept image.
elements.sent-2411-487: Random Recognition: To avoid repetitive ``Yes'' responses from the model, we randomly select 100 images from CC12M~\cite{changpinyo2021conceptual as inputs for the negative recognition Task.}
elements.sent-2411-488: Joint Recognition: Joint training not only helps in acquiring effective negative samples for a specific concept but also improves the model's ability to distinguish between concepts through inter-concept negative sampling.
elements.sent-2411-489: Furthermore, our experiments indicate that VQA performs better without affecting model's effectiveness in text-only conversations.

elements.sent-2411-490: We supplement the baselines not described in the main text:.
elements.sent-2411-491: LLaVA: Vanilla LLaVA~\cite{liu2024visual without any personalized information.}
elements.sent-2411-492: LLaVA+Prompt: We first prompt LLaVA to generate captions for all training images of a concept, then use these personalized captions in two ways: (I) concatenate all captions to form a comprehensive description of the concept; and (II) prompt LLaVA to summarize the captions into a concise, personalized description.
elements.sent-2411-493: During inference, we add $m$ relevant captions to the input to supply concept-specific information, where $m$ is the number of concepts evaluated.
elements.sent-2411-494: GPT4o+Prompt: Similar to LLaVA+Prompt, but using GPT-4o as the base model, which serves as an upper bound for downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-495: Notably, the GPT4o employed for testing differs from that used for data generation to avoid knowledge leakage.
elements.sent-2411-496: Question Answering All visual tasks share same data composition.
